messages.content:
Summarize the following text in four sentences or less.:
Johnson & Johnson must pay $18.8 million to California cancer patient in baby powder suit
83
Reuters
Tue, July 18, 2023 at 8:30 PM GMT-4·3 min read

Brendan McDermid/Reuters/File
Johnson & Johnson must pay $18.8 million to a California man who said he developed cancer from exposure to its baby powder, a jury decided on Tuesday, a setback for the company as it seeks to settle thousands of similar cases over its talc-based products in US bankruptcy court.

The jury ruled in favor of Emory Hernandez Valadez, who filed suit last year in California state court in Oakland against J&J, seeking monetary damages. Hernandez, 24, has said he developed mesothelioma, a deadly cancer, in the tissue around his heart as a result of heavy exposure to the company’s talc since childhood. The six-week trial was the first over talc that New Brunswick, New Jersey-based J&J has faced in almost two years.

The jury found that Hernandez was entitled to damages to compensate him for his medical bills and pain and suffering, but declined to award punitive damages against the company. Hernandez will not be able to collect the judgment in the foreseeable future, thanks to a bankruptcy court order freezing most litigation over J&J’s talc.

J&J vice president of litigation Erik Haas said in a statement that the company would appeal the verdict, calling it “irreconcilable with the decades of independent scientific evaluations confirming Johnson’s Baby Powder is safe, does not contain asbestos and does not cause cancer.”

A lawyer for Hernandez could not immediately be reached for comment.

Reuters watched the trial through Courtroom View Network.

In closing arguments to the jury on July 10, J&J’s lawyers said there was no evidence either linking Hernandez’s kind of mesolthelioma to asbestos or proving that Hernandez was ever exposed to tainted talc. Hernandez’s lawyers during closing arguments accused J&J of a “despicable” decades-long coverup of asbestos contamination.

Hernandez testified in June, telling jurors that he would have avoided J&J’s talc if he had been warned that it contained asbestos, as his lawsuit alleges. Jurors heard from Hernandez’s mother, Anna Camacho, who said she used large amounts of J&J’s baby powder on her son when he was a baby and through childhood. She cried as she described Hernandez’s illness.

Tens of thousands of plaintiffs have sued, alleging that J&J’s baby powder and other talc products sometimes contained asbestos and caused ovarian cancer and mesothelioma. J&J has said its talc products are safe and do not contain asbestos, which has been linked to mesothelioma.

J&J subsidiary LTL Management in April filed for bankruptcy in Trenton, New Jersey, proposing to pay $8.9 billion to settle more than 38,000 lawsuits and prevent new cases from coming forward. It was the company’s second attempt to resolve talc claims in bankruptcy, after a federal appeals court rejected an earlier bid.

Most litigation has been halted during bankruptcy proceedings, but US Chief Bankruptcy Judge Michael Kaplan, who is overseeing LTL’s Chapter 11, let Hernandez’s trial proceed because he is expected to live only a short time.

Hernandez’s form of mesothelioma is extremely rare, making his case different from the vast majority pending against J&J.

Asbestos plaintiffs are seeking to have LTL’s latest bankruptcy filing dismissed. They have argued the filing was brought in bad faith to insulate the company from litigation.

J&J and LTL have argued that bankruptcy delivers settlement payouts to plaintiffs more fairly, efficiently and equitably than trial courts, which they have likened to a “lottery” in which some litigants get large awards and others nothing.

J&J said in bankruptcy court filings that the costs of its talc-related verdicts, settlements and legal fees have reached about $4.5 billion.

For more CNN news and newsletters create an account at CNN.com

Johnson & Johnson has been ordered to pay $18.8 million to a California man who claims that he developed cancer from exposure to its baby powder. The jury ruled in favor of the plaintiff, Emory Hernandez Valadez, who alleged that he developed mesothelioma as a result of heavy exposure to the talc since childhood. While the jury awarded damages for medical bills and pain and suffering, they declined to grant punitive damages. Hernandez will not be able to collect the judgment in the near future due to the freezing of litigation over J&J's talc in bankruptcy court.
Elaborate on that last sentence.

Due to a bankruptcy court order freezing most litigation over Johnson & Johnson's talc, Emory Hernandez Valadez, the plaintiff in the case, will not be able to collect the $18.8 million judgment at this time. This is part of a larger effort by Johnson & Johnson to settle thousands of similar cases over its talc-based products in bankruptcy court. The freezing of litigation means that Hernandez's case is currently on hold, along with many others, until the bankruptcy proceedings are resolved.
Elaborate on the "bankruptcy proceedings" part.

Johnson & Johnson subsidiary LTL Management filed for bankruptcy in April in an attempt to settle thousands of talc-related lawsuits. The bankruptcy proceedings are taking place in Trenton, New Jersey, and the company has proposed a settlement of $8.9 billion to resolve over 38,000 lawsuits and prevent new cases from emerging. The bankruptcy filing has led to a stay on most litigation, halting the progress of ongoing cases against J&J's talc products. Chief Bankruptcy Judge Michael Kaplan allowed Hernandez's trial to proceed since his condition is expected to be terminal. However, for other plaintiffs, their cases are currently on hold until the bankruptcy proceedings come to a resolution.
So, if LTL is rendered bankrupt, does that mean Johson & Johnson will be off the hook for a lot of payments?

If LTL Management is rendered bankrupt, it does not mean that Johnson & Johnson will be completely off the hook for payments related to talc-related lawsuits. Bankruptcy proceedings allow companies to reorganize their debts and liabilities, but it does not absolve them of legal responsibility. In this case, Johnson & Johnson is the parent company of LTL and may still be held liable for the claims. The bankruptcy filing is an attempt by Johnson & Johnson to manage and resolve the numerous lawsuits more efficiently. However, the ultimate outcome and financial obligations will depend on the resolution of the bankruptcy proceedings and any settlements or judgments that may arise from it.
Does this mean that LTL will be sunset, though?

If LTL Management is rendered bankrupt, it is possible that the company may cease operations and be dissolved. Bankruptcy proceedings can involve the restructuring or liquidation of a company's assets to repay creditors. In this case, LTL's bankruptcy filing is aimed at resolving talc-related lawsuits and reaching a settlement with the plaintiffs. While the exact outcome and future of LTL are uncertain, bankruptcy often leads to significant changes in the company's operations, and it is possible that it may be dissolved or undergo a significant restructuring as part of the bankruptcy process.
Define "bankruptcy".

Bankruptcy is a legal process through which individuals or companies who are unable to repay their debts seek relief from their financial obligations. It typically involves filing a petition in court, which grants protection and relief to the debtor from creditors. The bankruptcy process can result in the reorganization of the debtor's finances, allowing them to create a plan to repay their debts over time, or it can lead to the liquidation of assets to satisfy the debts. The specific procedures and laws governing bankruptcy vary depending on the jurisdiction.
How would things play out different if bankruptcy weren't a thing/

If bankruptcy were not a legal option, the consequences for individuals or companies facing insurmountable debts would be significant. Without bankruptcy protection, debtors would not have a structured process to address their financial difficulties. They would likely have limited options, such as negotiating with creditors individually or attempting to repay the debts using personal assets or income. Without bankruptcy, the insurmountable debts could lead to prolonged financial distress, legal battles, and potentially the complete liquidation of assets. Bankruptcy provides a way for debtors to restructure their debts or seek a fresh start, offering a more systematic and regulated approach to resolving financial crises.
Provide some examples of what it means for debtors to "restructure their debts".

Restructuring debts refers to the process of modifying the terms of existing debt obligations to make them more manageable for the debtor. Some examples of debt restructuring mechanisms include:

1. Debt Consolidation: Combining multiple debts into a single loan with lower interest rates or longer repayment terms. This helps simplify the repayment process and potentially lowers monthly payments.

2. Loan Modification: Negotiating with creditors to modify the terms of an existing loan, such as reducing interest rates, extending the repayment period, or changing the payment schedule to make it more affordable.

3. Debt Settlement: Negotiating with creditors to pay a reduced amount to settle the debt, often through a lump sum payment or installment plan. This allows the debtor to satisfy the debt for less than the original amount owed.

4. Debt Repayment Plan: Setting up a structured repayment plan with a credit counseling agency. The agency works with the debtor and creditors to establish a single monthly payment based on the debtor's financial capabilities.

5. Chapter 11 Bankruptcy (for businesses): This form of bankruptcy allows businesses to restructure their debts while continuing to operate. It involves proposing a plan to repay creditors over time, potentially reducing debts and renegotiating contracts or leases.

These are just a few examples of how debtors can restructure their debts depending on their financial situation and available options. The aim is to find a more manageable way to repay debts, relieve financial stress, and regain control over finances.
Who provides permission for this to pass? The judge(s)? To the creditors have a say?

In the context of bankruptcy proceedings, the ultimate decision on debt restructuring is typically made by a judge, who approves or rejects the proposed plan. The judge oversees the case and evaluates the feasibility of the proposed repayment plan or debt restructuring arrangement.

Creditors also have a significant say in the process. They are given the opportunity to participate in the proceedings and provide their input on the proposed plan. Creditors can vote on whether to accept or reject the proposed terms of the debt restructuring. The level of creditor involvement and influence can vary depending on the specific type of bankruptcy and the jurisdiction's laws.

The judge takes into consideration the votes and opinions of creditors, along with other factors, to make an informed decision. The goal is to achieve a fair and reasonable outcome that balances the interests of both the debtor and the creditors.
Is it possible frequently enough to not be rare that a judge may proceed to do what the debor wants even if all creditors are unsatisfied?

In bankruptcy proceedings, the ultimate goal is to reach a fair and equitable resolution for all parties involved, including the debtor and the creditors. While the debtor's wishes and interests are considered, the court's primary responsibility is to uphold the principles of fairness and protect the rights of all parties, including the creditors.

In rare cases, a judge might approve a debtor's proposed plan that creditors oppose, particularly if there are extraordinary circumstances or compelling reasons that justify such a decision. However, it's important to note that bankruptcy proceedings are structured to ensure a fair and equitable process. The court will generally prioritize the rights and interests of creditors and seek to strike a balance between the debtor's needs and the reasonable expectations of the creditors.

Ultimately, the outcome can vary depending on the specific circumstances, the jurisdiction's laws, and the judge's discretion. However, it is typically expected that a resolution that considers the interests of both the debtor and the creditors will be sought.
